div.head: THE FORTUNATE ISLES, AND THEIR VNION. CELEBRATED IN A MASQVE Deſign'd for the Court, on the Twelfth night. 1626.
THE FORTVNATE ISLES.
div.stage: His Mtie being ſet, ENtreth in, running, JOHPHIEL, an aêry ſpirit, and (according to the Magi) the Intelligence of Jupiters ſphere: Attired in light ſilkes of ſeverall colours, with wings of the ſame, a bright yellow haire, a chaplet of flowers, blew ſilke ſtockings, and pumpes, and gloves, with a ſilver fan in his hand.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Like a lightning from the skie,
Or an arrow ſhot by Love,
Or a Bird of his let fly;
Bee't a Sparrow, or a Dove:
With that winged haſt, come I,
Looſed from the Sphere of Iove,
To wiſh good-night
To your delight.

div.stage: To him enters a Melancholique Student, in bare and worne cloathes, ſhrowded under an obſcure cloake, and the eaves of an old hat, fetching a deepe ſigh, his name, Mr. Mere-Foole.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Oh, oh!

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: In Saturn's name, the Father of my Lord!
What over-charged piece of Melancholie
Is this, breakes in betweene my wiſhes thus,
With bombing ſighes?

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: No! no Intelligence!
Not yet! and all my vowes now nine dayes old!
Blindneſſe of fate! Puppies had ſeene by this time:
But I ſee nothing! that I ſhould! or would ſee!
What meane the Brethren of the Roſie-Croſſe
So to deſert their votarie!

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: O! 'tis one
Hath vow'd himſelfe unto that aërie order,
And now is gaping for the flie they promis'd him.
I'le mixe a little with him for my ſport.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Have I both in my lodging, and my dyet,
My cloathes, and every other ſolemne charge
Obſerv'd'hem! made the naked bords my bed!
A fagot for my pillow! hungred ſore!

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: And thirſted after 'hem!

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: To looke gaunt, and leane!

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Which will not be.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: (Who's that?) yes, and outwatcht,
Yea, and out-walked any Ghoſt alive
In ſolitarie circle, worne my bootes,
Knees, armes, and elbowes out!

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Ran on the ſcore!

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: That have I (who ſuggeſts that?) and for more
Then I will ſpeake of, to abate this fleſh,
And have not gaind the ſight;

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Nay ſcarce the ſenſe.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: (Voice, thou art right) of any thing but a cold
Wind in my ſtomacke.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: And a kind of whimſie.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Here in my head, that puts me to the ſtaggers,
Whether there be that Brotherhood, or no.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Beleeve fraile man, they be: and thou ſhalt ſee.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: What ſhall I ſee?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Mee.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Thee? Where?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Here. If you
Be Mr. Mere-Foole.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Sir, our name is Mery-Foole.
But by contraction Mere-foole.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Then are you
The wight I ſeeke: and Sr. my name is Jophiel,
Intelligence to the Sphere of Jupiter,
An aëry jocular ſpirit, employ'd to you
From Father OVTIS.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: OVTIS? who is hee?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Know yee not OVTIS? Then you know No body:
The good old Hermit, that was ſaid to dwell
Here in the forreſt without trees, that built
The Caſtle in the aire, where all the Brethren
Rhodoſtaurotick live. It flyes with wings,
And runnes on wheeles: where Julian de Campis
Holds out the brandiſht blade.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Is't poſſible
They thinke on mee?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Riſe, be not loſt in wonder,
But heare me, and be faithfull. All the Brethren
Have heard your vowes, ſalute you, and expect you,
By me, this next returne. But the good Father
Has bin content to die for you.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: For mee?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: For you. Laſt New-yeares day, which ſome give out,
Becauſe it was his Birth-day, and began
The yeare of Jubile, he would reſt upon it,
Being his hundred five and twentieth yeare:
But the truth is, having obſerv'd your Geneſis,
He would not live, becauſe he might leave all
He had to you.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: What had he?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Had? An office,
Two, three, or foure.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Where?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: In the upper Region:
And that you'll find. The Farme of the great Cuſtomes,
Through all the Ports of the Aires Intelligences;
Then Conſtable of the Caſtle Roſie-Croſſe:
Which you muſt be, and Keeper of the Keyes
Of the whole Kaball, with the Seales; you ſhall be
Principall Secretarie to the Starres;
Know all their ſignatures, and combinations,
The divine rods, and conſecrated roots.
What not? Would you turne trees up like the wind,
To ſhew your ſtrength? march ouer heads of armies,
Or points of pikes, to ſhew your lightneſſe? force
All doores of arts, with the petarre, of your wit?
Reade at one view all bookes? ſpeake all the languages
Of ſeverall creatures? maſter all the learnings
Were, are, or ſhall be? or, to ſhew your wealth,
Open all treaſures, hid by nature, from
The rocke of Diamond, to the mine of Sea-coale?
Sir, you ſhall doe it.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: But how?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Why, by his skill,
Of which he has left you the inheritance,
Here in a pot: this little gally pot
Of tincture, high roſe tincture. There's your Order,
You will ha' your Collar ſent you, er't be long.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: I lookt Sir, for a halter, I was deſperate.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Reach forth your hand.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: O Sir, a broken ſleeve
Keepes the arme back as 'tis i'the proverbe.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Nay,
For that I doe commend you: you muſt be poore
With all your wealth, and learning. When you ha'made
Your glaſſes, gardens in the depth of Winter,
Where you will walke inviſible to Man-kind,
Talkt with all birds and beaſts in their owne language,
When you have penetrated hills like ayre,
Div'd to the bottome of the Sea, like leade.
And riſs' againe like corke, walk't in the fire
An 'twere a Salamander, paſs'd through all
The winding orbes, like an Intelligence,
Up to the Empyreum, when you have made
The World your gallery, can diſpatch a buſineſſe
In ſome three minutes, with the Antipodes,
And in five more, negotiate the Globe over;
You muſt be poore ſtill.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: By my place, I know it.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Where would you wiſh to be now? or what to ſee?
Without the fortunate purſe to beare your charges,
Or wiſhing hat? I will but touch your temples,
The corners of your eyes, and tinct the tip,
The very tip o' your noſe, with this Collyrium
And you ſhall ſee i'the ayre all the Idea's,
Spirits, and Atomes, Flies, that buz about
This way, and that way, and are rather admirable,
Then any way intelligible.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: O, come, tinct me,
Tinct me: I long, ſave this great belly, I long.
But ſhall I onely ſee?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: See, and command
As they were all your varlets, or your foot-boyes:
But firſt you muſt declare, (your greatneſſe muſt,
For that is now your ſtile) what you would ſee.
Or whom.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Is that my ſtile? My Greatneſſe, then,
Would ſee King Zoroaſtres.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Why you ſhall:
Or any one beſide. Thinke whom you pleaſe?
Your thouſand, Your ten thouſand, to a million:
All's one to me, if you could name a myriad.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: I have nam'd him.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: You'ave reaſon.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: I, I have reaſon.
Becauſe he's ſaid to be the Father of conjurers,
And a cunning man i'the ſtarres.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: I, that's it troubles us.
A little for the preſent: For, at this time
He is confuting a French Almanack,
But he will ſtraight have done, Ha' you but patience;
Or thinke but any other in meane time,
Any hard name.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Then, Hermes Triſmegiſtus.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l:
O,
〈 in non-Latin alphabet 〉 ? Why, you ſhall ſee him,
A fine hard name. Or him, or whom you will,
As I ſaid to you afore. Or what doe you thinke
Of Howle-glaſſe, in ſtead of him.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: No, him
I have a mind to.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: O', but Vlen-ſpiegle.
Were ſuch a name! but you ſhall have your longing.
What lucke is this, he ſhould be buſie too?
He is waighing water, but to fill three houre-glaſſes,
And marke the day in pen' orths like a cheeſe,
And he has done. 'Tis ſtrange you ſhould name him
Of all the reſt! there being Jamblicus,
Or Porphyrie, or Proclus, any name
That is not buſie.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Let me ſee Pythagoras.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Good.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Or Plato.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Plato, is framing ſome Idea's,
Are now beſpoken, at a groat a dozen,
Three groſſe at leaſt: And, for Pythagoras,
He' has raſhly run himſelfe on an imployment,
Of keeping Aſſes from a field of beanes;
And cannot be ſtav'd off.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Then, Archimedes.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Yes, Archimedes!

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: I, or Aeſope.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Nay,
Hold your firſt man, a good man, Archimedes,
And worthy to be ſeene; but he is now
Inventing a rare Mouſe-trap with Owles wings
And a Catts-foot, to catch the Miſe alone:
And Aeſop, he is filing a Fox tongue,
For a new fable he has made of Court;
But you ſhall ſee'hem all, ſtay but your time
And aske in ſeaſon; Things as'kd out of ſeaſon
A man denies himſelfe. At ſuch a time
As Chriſtmas, when diſguiſing is o'foot,
To aske of the inventions, and the men,
The witts, and the ingines that move thoſe Orbes!
Me thinkes, you ſhould enquire now, after Skelton,
Or Mr. Skogan.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Skogan? what was he?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: O' a fine Gentleman, and a Maſter of Arts,
Of Henry the fourth's times, that made diſguiſes
For the Kings ſonnes, and writ in ballad-royall
Daintily well.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: But, wrote he like a Gentleman?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: In rime! fine tinckling rime! and flow and verſe!
With now and then ſome ſence! and he was paid for't,
Regarded, and rewarded: which few Poets
Are now adaies.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: And why?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: 'Cauſe every Dabler
In rime is thought the ſame. But you ſhall ſee him.
Hold up your noſe.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: I had rather ſee a Brachman,
Or a Gymnoſophiſt yet.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: You ſhall ſee him, Sir.
Is worth them both. And with him Domine Skelton,
The worſhipfull Poet Laureat to K. Harry,
And Tytire tu of thoſe times. Advance quick Skogan,
And quicker Skelton, ſhew your craftie heads,
Before this Heire of arts, this Lord of learning,
This Maſter of all knowledge in reverſion.

div.stage: Enter SKOGAN, and SKELTON in like habits, as they liv'd.

sp.speaker: SKOGAN.
sp.l: Seemeth we are call'd of a morall intent,
If the words that are ſpoken, as well now be meant.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: That Mr. Skogan I dare you enſure.

sp.speaker: SKOGAN.
sp.l: Then, Sonne, our acquaintance is like to indure.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: A pretty game! like Crambe. Mr. Skogan,
Give me thy hand: Thou'rt very leane, me thinks,
Is't living by thy wits?

sp.speaker: SKOGAN.
sp.l: If it had beene that,
My worſhipfull Sonne, thou hadſt ne're bin ſo fat.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: He tels you true Sir. Here's a Gentleman
(My paire of crafty Clerkes) of that high caract,
As hardly hath the age produc't his like.
Who not content with the wit of his owne times,
Is curious to know yours, and what hath beene,

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: Or is, or ſhall be.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Note his Latitude!

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: The queſtion-iſsimus
Is, ſhould he aske a ſight now, for his life;
I meane, a perſon, he would have reſtor'd,
To memorie of theſe times, for a Play-fellow,
Whether you would preſent him, with an Hermes,
Or, with an Howle-glas?

sp.speaker: SKELTON.
sp.l: An Howleglaſſe
To come, to paſſe
On his Fathers Aſſe;
There never was,
By day, nor night,
A finer ſight.
With feathers upright
In his horned cap,
And crooked ſhape,
Much like an Ape.
With Owle on fiſt,
And Glaſſe at his wriſt.

sp.speaker: SKOGAN.
sp.l: Except the foure Knaves entertain'd for the guards,
Of the Kings, and the Queenes that triumph in the cards.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL
sp.l: I, that were a ſight and a halfe, I confeſſe,
To ſee 'hem come skipping in, all at a meſſe!

sp.speaker: SKELTON.
sp.l: With Elinor Rumming.
To make up the mumming,
That comely Gill,
That dwelt on a hill,
But ſhe is not grill:
Her face all bowſie,
Droopie, and drowſie,
Scurvy, and lowſie,
Comely crinkled,
Wondrouſly wrinkled,
Like a roſt pigs eare,
Briſtled with haire.

sp.speaker: SCOGAN.
sp.l: Or, what doe you ſay to Ruffian Fitz-Ale?

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: An excellent ſight, if he be not to ſtale.
But then, we can mix him with moderne Vapors,
The Child of Tobacco, his pipes, and his papers.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: You talk'd of Elinor Rumming, I had rather
See Ellen of Troy.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Her you ſhall ſee.
But credit mee,
That Marie Ambree
(Who march'd ſo free.
To the ſiege of Gaunt,
And death could not daunt,
As the Ballad doth vaunt)
Were a braver wight,
And a better ſight.

sp.speaker: SKELTON.
sp.l: Or Weſtminſter Meg,
With her long leg,
As long as a Crane;
And feet like a plane:
With a paire of heeles,
As broad as two wheeles;
To drive downe the dew,
As ſhe goes to the ſtew:
And turnes home merry,
By Lambeth Ferry.
Or you may have come
In, Thomas Thumbe,
In a pudding fatt
With Doctor Ratt.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: I, that! that! that!
Wee'll have'em all,
To fill the Hall.

div.stage: Conſiſting of theſe twelve perſons, Owleglaſſe, the foure Knaves, two Ruffians, Fitz-ale, and Vapore, Elnor Rumming, Mary Ambree, Long-Meg of Weſtminſter, Tom Thumbe, and Doctor Ratt.

sp.speaker: MERE-FOOLE.
sp.l: What! are they vaniſh'd! where is skipping Skelton?
Or morall Skogan? I doe like their ſhew
And would have thankt'em, being the firſt grace
The Company of the Roſie-Croſſe hath done me.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: The company o'the Roſie-Croſſe! you wigion,
The company of Players. Goe, you are,
And will be ſtill your ſelfe, a Mere-foole, In;
And take your pot of honey here, and hogs greace,
See, who has guld you, and make one. Great King,
Your pardon, if deſire to pleaſe have treſpaſs'd.
This foole ſhould have beene ſent to Antycira,
(The Ile of Ellebore) there to have purg'd,
Not hop'd a happie ſeat within your waters.
Heare now the meſſage of the Fates, and Jove,
On whom thoſe Fates depend, to you, as Neptune
The great Commander of the Seas, and Iles.
That point of Revolution being come
When all the Fortunate Iſlands ſhould be joyn'd,
MACARIA, one, and thought a Principall,
That hitherto hath floted, as uncertaine
Where ſhe ſhould fix her bleſſings, is to night
Inſtructed to adhere to your BRITANNIA:
That where the happie ſpirits live, hereafter
Might be no queſtion made, by the moſt curious,
Since the Macarij come to doe you homage,
And joyne their cradle to your continent.

div.stage: Here the Scene opens, and the Maſquers are diſcover'd ſitting in their ſeverall ſeiges. The ayre opens above, and APOLLO with Harmony, and the ſpirits of Muſique ſing, the while the Iland moves forward, Proteus ſitting below, and hearkening.

div.head: SONG.
div.l: Looke forth the Shepheard of the Seas,
And of the Ports that keepe the keyes,
MACARIA, Prince of all the Iſles,
Wherein there nothing growes, but ſmiles,
Doth here put in, to dwell.
The windes are ſweet, and gently blow,
But Zephirus, no breath they know,
By him the virgin violets live,
And every plant doth odours give,
As new, as are the howers.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: Then, thinke it not a common cauſe,
That to it ſo much wonder drawes,
And all the heavens conſent,
With Harmony to tune their notes,
In anſwer to the publike votes,
That for it up were ſent.

div.stage: By this time, the Iland having joyned it ſelfe to the ſhore; PROTEVS, PORTVNVS, and SARON come forth, and goe up ſinging to the State, while the Maſquers take time to ranke themſelves.

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: I, now, the heights of Neptunes honours ſhine,
And all the glories of his greater ſtile
Are read, reflected in this happieſt Ile.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: How both the ayre, the ſoyle, the ſeat combine
To ſpeake it bleſſed!

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: Theſe are the true groves,
Where joyes are borne.

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Where longings,

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: And where loves!

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: That live!

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: That laſt!

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: No intermitted wind
Blowes here, but what leaves flowers, or fruit behind.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: 'Tis odour all, that comes!
And every tree doth give his gummes.

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: There is no ſickneſſe, nor no old age knowne
To man, nor any griefe that hee dares owne.
There is no hunger there, nor envy of ſtate.
Nor leaſt ambition in the Magiſtrate.
But all are even-hearted, open, free,
And what one is, another ſtrives to be.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: Here all the day, they feaſt, they ſport, and ſpring;
Now dance the Graces Hay; now Venus Ring:
To which the old Muſitians play, and ſing.

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: There is ARION, tuning his bold Harpe,
from flat to ſharpe.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: And light Anacreon,
He ſtill is one!

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Steſichorus there, too,
That Linus, and old Orpheus doth out-doe
To wonder.

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: And Amphion! he is there.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: Nor is Apollo dainty to appeare
In ſuch a quire, although the trees be thick,

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: He will looke in, and ſee the aires be quick,
And that the times be true.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: Then, chanting,

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Then,
Up, with their notes, they raiſe the Prince of Men.

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: And ſing the preſent Prophecie that goes
Of joyning the bright LILLIE, and the ROSE.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: See! all the flowers

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: That ſpring the banks along,
Doe move their heads unto that under-ſong.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: SARON, PORTVNVS, PROTEVS, helpe to bring
Our Primroſe in, the glory of the ſpring!
And tell the Daffadill, againſt that day,
That we prepare new Gyrlands freſh as May.
And enter-weave the Myrtle, and the Bay.

div.stage: This ſung, the Iſland goes backe, whil'ſt the upper Chorus takes it from them, and the Maſquers prepare for their figure.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: Spring all the Graces of the age,
And all the Loves of time;
Bring all the pleaſures of the ſtage,
And reliſhes of rime:
Adde all the ſoftneſſes of Courts,
The lookes, the laughters, and the ſports.
And mingle all their ſweets, and ſalts,
That none may ſay, the Triumph halts.

div.stage: The Maſquers Dance their Entry or firſt dance. Which done, the firſt Proſpective, a Maritime Palace, or the houſe of Oceanus is diſcovered to loude Muſicke. The other above is no more ſeene.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.
sp.l: Behold the Palace of Oceanus!

l.hi: Hayle Reverend ſtructure! Boaſt no more to us
Thy being able, all the Gods to feaſt;
We ſaw enough: when ALBION was thy gueſt.

div.stage: The Meaſures. After which, the ſecond Proſpective, a Sea is ſhowne, to the former Muſicke.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.

l.hi: Now turne; and view the wonders of the deepe,

sp.l: Where Proteus Herds, and Neptunes Orkes doe keepe,

l.hi: Where all is plough'd, yet ſtill the paſtures greene
New wayes are found, and yet no paths are ſeene.

div.stage: Here Proteus, Portunus, Saron, goe up to the Ladyes with this Song.

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Come noble Nymphs, and doe not hide
The joyes, for which you ſo provide:

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: If not to mingle with the Men,
What doe you here? Goe home agen.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: Your dreſsings doe confeſſe,
By what we ſee, ſo curious parts
Of Pallas, and Arachnes arts,
That you could meane no leſſe.

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Why doe you weare the Silk-wormes toyles,
Or glory in the ſhell-fiſh ſpoyles;
Or ſtrive to ſhew the graines of Ore
That you have gather'd on the ſhore,
whereof to make a ſtocke
To graft the greener Emerald on,
Or any better water'd ſtone,

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: Or Rubie of the Rocke?

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Why doe you ſmell of Amber-gris,
Of which was formed Neptunes Neice,
The Queene of Love: unleſſe you can
Like Sea-borne Venus love a Man?

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: Try, put your ſelves unto't.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: Your lookes, your ſmiles, and thoughts that meet,
Ambroſian hands, and ſilver feet,
Doe promiſe you will do't.
The Revells follow.
Which ended, the Fleet is diſcovered, while the
three Corners play.

sp.speaker: JOHPHIEL.

l.hi: 'Tis time, your eyes ſhould be refreſht at length
With ſomething new, a part of NEPTVNES ſtrength,
See yond', his Fleete, ready to goe or come,

sp.l: Or fetch the riches of the Ocean home,

l.hi: So to ſecure him, both in peace, and warres,
Till not one ſhip alone, but all be ſtarres.

sp.speaker: PROTEVS.
sp.l: Although we wiſh the glory ſtill might laſt
Of ſuch a night, and for the cauſes paſt:
Yet now, great Lord of waters, and of Iles,
Give Proteus leave to turne unto his wiles.

sp.speaker: PORTVNVS.
sp.l: And, whilſt young ALBION doth thy labours eaſe;
Diſpatch Portunus to the Ports.

sp.speaker: SARON.
sp.l: And Saron to the Seas:
To meet old Nereus, with his fiftie girles,
From aged Indus laden home with pearles,
And Orient gummes, to burne unto thy name.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: And may thy ſubjects hearts be all on flame,
Whil'ſt thou doſt keepe the earth in firme eſtate,
And 'mongſt the winds, do'ſt ſuffer no debate,
But both at Sea, and Land, our powers increaſe,
With health, and all the golden gifts of Peace.
